summary: Patrick details a highly productive weekend of coding on Bingo Card Creator version 2.5, triggered by a customer's UTF-8 bug report. The update includes several feature improvements: consistent column placement for words, a reworked Wizards menu supporting 500+ word lists, automatic update functionality for word lists, and smarter registration key handling. He also announces a partnership with Excelsior to test whether eliminating the Java dependency increases sales. The post serves as both a development log and a call for beta testers.
key_points: Customer bug reports can be catalysts for productive development sprints that go well beyond just fixing the bug
Investing in content (507 paid word lists) creates compounding value when the software can dynamically deliver that content to users
Building automatic update functionality turns an existing marketing expense (freelance writers) into double the value by eliminating manual rebuild cycles
A/B testing a native compilation versus Java dependency is a smart way to isolate whether technical friction costs sales
money_quotes: On Friday a customer reported a bug with UTF-8 files, which had creeped in literally months ago. Since that was blocking her from getting work done I had a fire lit under my behind this weekend, and spent all three days of it coding like mad.
I've paid for 507 (and counting) lists to be written, so why not ship them all?
Double the bang for the buck out of money I was spending on marketing anyhow.
I'm always willing to do an A/B test in the service of making me money, and if it helps them out so much the better.
Finally I join the 1960s of user interface design.
key_insight: A single customer bug report can spark a burst of development that compounds existing investments — turning 507 paid word lists and freelance writing spend into significantly more customer value through better software delivery.
